evidence_quotes.quote: This can suddenly be denied access to it.
A product that is sold and has single player modes should remain playable even after the servers have been switched off
The physical copy of which you have placed in his cupboard is now nothing more than a hard letter.

paragraphs.text: Ladies and gentlemen, in my free time, I love to immerse in retro games in order to create old childhood reminders or to experience hidden treasures for myself for the first time. For me, video games are considered works of art and works of art should not have an expiry date. Not everyone plays every game when it comes out. It takes months or even years before you want to get a title. In the then generation of video games, this was possible without difficulty. The physical copy of a SNES, dreamcast or PS2 game still works after decades as long as a little eight has been placed on the module or console. Finally, one paid for the title, it should be in the consumer’s discretion when he would like to play this title again. Unfortunately, with current practices, this is becoming increasingly difficult by mega-groups, even if you had already bought a physical copy of the game. This can suddenly be denied access to it. As an example, titles such as ‘The Crew’ by Ubisoft or ‘Darkspore’ of Electronic Arts. Both titles require a central server to connect to these titles, even if you want to enjoy only the single player campaign. The servers of these titles have already been switched off and the money invested in this game is now also away. The physical copy of which you have placed in his cupboard is now nothing more than a hard letter. I would like to ask you to put an end to these practices. A product that is sold and has single player modes should remain playable even after the servers have been switched off, so that the game itself is preserved and future generations can also enjoy this game, and so that a product purchased against bars cannot simply be taken away. In my view, this is linked to theft.
